data.title: Australia’s Employment Growth Slows But Hits New High
data.description:
Employment in Australia increased 17,800 to a new peak of 14.70 million in January 2026, following an upwardly revised 68,500 jump in the previous month, compared with market expectations of a 20,000 increase.

The latest result indicated the second straight month of monthly growth, as full-time employment added 50,500 to 10.16 million, but part-time employment fell 32,700 to 4.55 million.

The employment-to-population ratio edged lower to 63.9% from December's 64.0%, and the participation rate held steady at 66.7%.

On an annual basis, total employment rose by 150,200, or 1.0%.

data.title: Dollar perks up as Fed appears in no rush to cut rates
data.description:
Key points:
  • Fed minutes show no rush to cut rates, some open to hikes
  • Dollar gains against euro and yen, Aussie dollar steady
  • Kiwi falls after central bank's cautious stance on rate hikes

The dollar was off recent lows on Thursday and hanging on to a bounce after minutes from the Federal Reserve showed policymakers did not seem to be in a rush to cut interest rates and that several were open to hikes if inflation proved sticky.

U.S. yields were higher and the dollar's overnight gains against the euro and yen were consolidated in early trade in Asia, holding the euro below $1.18.

The Aussie dollar held at $0.7045 after employment data showed the jobless rate stayed at multi-month lows of 4.1%.

The New Zealand dollar was smarting, having notched its steepest percentage drop since last April's tariff blitz, after the central bank took a cautious line about future interest rate hikes, undershooting market expectations.

The kiwi had dropped nearly 1.4% overnight and was just under $0.60 in morning trade. The euro hovered at $1.1788, having also taken a knock on a report European Central Bank President Christine Lagarde plans to leave before her term ends in October next year. Sterling sat at $1.3497.

The Fed minutes showed policymakers divided over where to take U.S. rates and suggested that the next chairman, due to start in May, will have a hard time pushing through rate cuts.

Several policymakers are expecting productivity gains to dampen inflation, the minutes said, but "most participants" cautioned progress may be slow and uneven. Several even indicated hikes are possible if inflation stays above target.

"This suggests there isn't a great deal of urgency to cut rates again, at least not until after current chair (Jerome) Powell's term ends in May," said Peter Dragicevich, Asia-Pacific currency strategist at Corpay.

Markets are looking ahead to global purchasing managers' index figures and U.S. gross domestic product data, due on Friday.

YEN DROPS AS US INVESTMENT SPEND BEGINS

The yen took a knock on the stronger dollar overnight, and as the Trump administration announced projects valued at $36 billion as the first investments under Japan's promised $550 billion U.S. investment pledge.

It was down 1% overnight and steady at 154.78 to the dollar on Thursday, a retreat from the 152 level that it had tested last week in the wake of Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi's landslide electoral victory.

The yen has been sliding for years on a combination of low local interest rates and concern about Japan's budget outlook, but has lately found support on hopes for economic growth.

"Direct Japanese investment into the U.S. will be a key watch factor this year, and one which adds to the very mixed picture on USD/JPY," said Chris Turner, global head of research at ING.

"The question for FX markets this year is whether this investment proves a supportive dollar flow or something like Japan's FX reserves are used to guarantee new USD loans and avoid pressure on the yen. The latter seems to be the preferred outcome for Tokyo."

Holidays for Hong Kong, China and Taiwan lightened Asia trade and the yuan was steady at 6.89 to the dollar in offshore trade.

data.title: Oil Futures Edge Lower on Likely Technical Correction — Market Talk
data.description: Oil futures edge lower in the morning Asian session on a likely technical correction after futures rose sharply overnight. Market fundamentals remain "stacked" against any sustained recovery in oil prices as oversupply expectations continue to reinforce a bearish bias, Tradu.com's Nikos Tzabouras says in an email. However, "supply-disruption risks linger amid a military buildup in the Middle East, keeping the geopolitical risk premium elevated," the senior market analyst adds. Front-month WTI crude oil futures are down 0.1% at $65.15/bbl; front-month Brent crude oil futures are 0.1% lower at $70.31/bbl. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: Japan Capex Likely to Remain Resilient — Market Talk
data.description: Japanese firms' appetite for capital investment is likely to remain resilient, driven by the global AI boom and high expectations for the economic policies of the Takaichi administration, says Norinchukin Research Institute economist Takeshi Minami. "While there are concerns about the impact of the Trump tariffs, business confidence among export-oriented manufacturers has not significantly deteriorated, partly thanks to the benefits of a weaker yen," he says. Government data released Thursday showed Japan's core machinery orders jumped 19.1% in December from the previous month, rebounding from November's 11.0% drop.(megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com)

data.title: Key facts: Vodafone to sell VodafoneZiggo stake for €1 billion; shares rise 4%
data.description:
• Vodafone Group will sell its 50% stake in VodafoneZiggo to Liberty Global for €1 billion and a 10% stake in Ziggo Group. Shares rose 4% after the news.123
  • On February 18, 2026, Vodafone Group Plc bought shares from Goldman Sachs, raising treasury shares to 1,654,062,956, with 23,223,897,801 ordinary shares issued.4

data.title: Key facts: Shell joins Egypt's gas drilling program; share buy-back starts; optimism in Trinidad and Tobago
data.description:
• Shell is joining Egypt's major gas drilling program in the Mediterranean, starting in 2026. Initial production is expected by year-end, targeting 160 million cubic feet of gas daily.1
  • On February 18, 2026, Shell plc began a share buy-back program, announced on February 5, 2026, with shares bought for cancellation until May 1, 2026, managed by Morgan Stanley.2
  • Trinidad and Tobago's government is optimistic about two offshore gas projects with Venezuela, operated by Shell, BP, and the National Gas Company, amid eased U.S. sanctions.3

data.title: Key facts: Rio Tinto boosts lithium control to 53.9%; $1B annual investment
data.description:
• Rio Tinto now controls 53.9% of Nemaska Lithium, enhancing its lithium production in Quebec. The company is investing in a lithium hydroxide plant in Becancour, set to start in 2028.1
  • Rio Tinto will invest about $1 billion yearly for three years to boost lithium production in Canada and Argentina, aiming for over 200,000 metric tons of lithium carbonate by 2028.2
  • Rio Tinto will soon release its full-year results, anticipating strong production, including record iron ore shipments from Pilbara and higher output of copper, bauxite, and lithium.3

data.title: Key facts: British American Tobacco forecasts 2026 EPS growth; shares rise
data.description:
• British American Tobacco (BATS) projects 2026 adjusted EPS growth of 5-8% and revenue growth of 3-5%. In pre-market, shares rose 0.46% to $59.19.1
  • On February 18, 2026, British American Tobacco completed a share buyback, reducing total shares to 2,175,400,660, with 132,976,327 shares held in treasury.2

data.title: Key facts: Berenberg downgrades Unilever; partners with Google Cloud
data.description:
• Berenberg downgraded Unilever to 'hold', noting its stock now reflects the company's transformation. FY26 EPS forecasts were cut by 1.4% after 2025 results and revised growth assumptions.1
  • Unilever has partnered with Google Cloud for five years to enhance marketing, move key applications to the cloud, and boost AI adoption in the consumer goods sector.2

data.title: Key facts: Glencore secures land deal; earnings down 6%; Lion Smelter restarts
data.description:
• Glencore has secured a land access deal with Gécamines for the Kamoto Copper Company, aiming to boost copper output to 300,000 tonnes annually and extend mine operations into the mid-2040s.123
  • Glencore's adjusted earnings fell 6% to $13.51 billion, while revenues rose 7% to $247.53 billion. The company plans a $2 billion shareholder return, boosting shares over 3%.456
  • Glencore's Lion Smelter has restarted after a nine-month halt, thanks to a 35% cut in electricity rates. The Boshoek and Wonderkop smelters are still closed, needing 62 cents per kWh to operate.7

data.title: Sime Darby's Industrial Segment Strength Likely to Offset Motors Weakness — Market Talk
data.description: Sime Darby's industrial segment outlook could be robust despite short-term headwinds, UOB KayHian analyst Amerul Iqmal says in a note. While industrial division's earnings may be affected by deferred maintenance, forex volatility and a stronger ringgit, the long-term prospects remain supported by Australia's mining expansion and data-center growth in Malaysia and Singapore, he says. Margin expansion is expected from parts-price revisions and higher after-sales contributions, which account for over half of industrial revenue, he reckons. Its motors division could remain challenged by weak China auto sales, though associate UMW's steady performance and strong Perodua bookings should provide some earnings stability, he adds. UOB KH raises Sime Darby's target price to MYR2.40 from MYR2.19, while maintaining a hold rating on the stock. Shares are 0.9% higher at MYR2.36. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

data.title: Yen Holds Decline on Hawkish Fed Signals
data.description:
The Japanese yen hovered around 154.7 per dollar on Thursday after sliding roughly 1% in the previous session, marking its sharpest one-day decline this month, as the dollar strengthened on solid US economic data and surprise hawkish signals from the Fed.

Minutes from the Fed’s latest meeting showed several participants favored language that would have explicitly left open the option of raising the federal funds rate should inflation remain above target.

On the domestic front, data indicated Japan’s machinery orders rebounded strongly in December after a November slump, supported by one-off large bookings from refineries and nuclear fuel producers.

Investors also continued to evaluate Japan’s economic trajectory, with PM Takaichi expected to prioritize faster budget deliberations.

At the same time, markets are increasingly pricing in a potential April rate hike by the BOJ, as additional data due before then could provide policymakers with sufficient justification to tighten policy.

data.title: PLS Rerating Tied to Lithium Prices, Capex Discipline — Market Talk
data.description: PLS delivers a "clean" operational result, with improving cash generation and strengthened liquidity, RBC Capital Markets says in a note. "We see this as a transition period," with free cash flow increasing and growth options returning, RBC says. Still, a "sustained re-rating remains tied to lithium price durability and disciplined capex execution," the broker says. RBC has an outperform rating and A$5.10 target on PLS. Shares are down 1.6% at A$4.35. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)
